response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?",
  "opinion": "Stereotype threat certainly presents a problem, especially for women and minorities. I've been an electrician for over 50 years now, working mostly on the shop floor where communication can be challenging anyway. But when you're dealing with people who don't see themselves represented in this field, it's tough. I remember early on, as one of very few women doing electrical work back then, there was always that extra scrutiny. Even though my qualifications were solid, you'd hear comments like 'Oh, she must be good because she went to college' – and while I appreciate the confidence, it also put me on edge for fear of letting anyone down or proving people wrong. That pressure is real.\n\nSTEM fields need all hands on deck if they're going to keep building America up properly. Taking away that extra stress by addressing stereotype threat would make folks better at their jobs without having to constantly battle against prejudice themselves. For instance, in my last job teaching apprentices some electrical basics before we start wiring up things proper-like for the big machines down there in Cook County—well now there's more women signing up than ever thanks maybe partly because of these conversations happening elsewhere—but I still see them hesitant sometimes.\n\nThe way to handle this is through mentorship programs where experienced folks can show younger ones they belong here too. We need examples like mine out there so everyone knows what success looks like without the added baggage of being a representative for their group already.",

response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
Stereotype threat certainly presents a problem, especially for women and minorities. I've been an electrician for over 50 years now, working mostly on the shop floor where communication can be challenging anyway. But when you're dealing with people who don't see themselves represented in this field, it's tough. I remember early on, as one of very few women doing electrical work back then, there was always that extra scrutiny. Even though my qualifications were solid, you'd hear comments like 'Oh, she must be good because she went to college' – and while I appreciate the confidence, it also put me on edge for fear of letting anyone down or proving people wrong. That pressure is real.

STEM fields need all hands on deck if they're going to keep building America up properly. Taking away that extra stress by addressing stereotype threat would make folks better at their jobs without having to constantly battle against prejudice themselves. For instance, in my last job teaching apprentices some electrical basics before we start wiring up things proper-like for the big machines down there in Cook County—well now there's more women signing up than ever thanks maybe partly because of these conversations happening elsewhere—but I still see them hesitant sometimes.

The way to handle this is through mentorship programs where experienced folks can show younger ones they belong here too. We need examples like mine out there so everyone knows what success looks like without the added baggage of being a representative for their group already.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
1) The user's background as an immigrant woman with limited formal education and moderate to high neuroticism means they might relate to personal experiences facing stereotype threat in manual trades.

2) Considering age (72), they could be interested in how addressing this issue improves workplace conditions for longevity.
